title: Japan’s ‘Invest More At Home’ Plan
text:
Japan’s ‘Invest More At Home’ Plan

Udith Sikand

Gavekal Research | 14 Jul 2026

On Friday, after Japanese finance minister Satsuki Katayama said the government would encourage domestic institutions to invest more domestically, the yen strengthened, with the US dollar-yen exchange rate falling by one big figure from ¥162.40 to ¥161.40. Then on Monday, as market participants realized that the government had no immediate plans either to strong-arm institutions to increase their allocations to domestic assets, or to offer incentives for them to do so, the yen weakened again, with the US dollar trading back at ¥162.30 Tuesday in Asia. So should Katayama’s words be disregarded entirely?Maybe not. Since Japan’s late prime minister Shinzo Abe launched his “Abenomics” economic revitalization plan in 2012, the yen has depreciated by more than -50% against the US dollar. This currency weakness is generally attributed to the ultra-loose monetary policies pursued by the Bank of Japan in support of the Abenomics agenda. Fair enough. But a large part of the mechanism at work was capital outflows resulting from increased allocations to overseas asset markets by Japanese investors.
